message.content:
Here is a checklist-style enumeration of **Google Search Central–documented, testable hreflang / i18n SEO requirements**, with each item mapped to:

- the **check** (what you test),
- **what it verifies**, and
- the **exact Google Search Central topic and URL**.

I focus only on **Google’s own docs**, primarily:

- Localized versions of your pages  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]  
- Managing multi‑regional and multilingual sites  
  `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/managing-multi-regional-sites`[6]

---

## 1. Hreflang annotation presence and placement

### 1.1. Hreflang present on all localized variants

- **Check**  
  For every URL that is part of a language/region variant set, verify that **hreflang annotations exist** in one of the supported implementations (HTML `<link>`, HTTP header, or XML sitemap) and that **every variant uses hreflang**.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms that Google can recognize that the pages are **localized variations of the same content** and can select the appropriate version in search results.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “Use hreflang to tell Google about the variations of your content”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 1.2. Hreflang in HTML `<head>` only (when using HTML implementation)

- **Check**  
  If using HTML, verify that all `<link rel="alternate" hreflang="...">` tags are:
  - inside a **well‑formed `<head>`** section, and
  - not duplicated elsewhere (for example, in `<body>`).

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that hreflang annotations in HTML are **parseable and valid** by Google’s standards.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “The `<link>` tags must be inside a well‑formed `<head>` section of the HTML.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 1.3. Supported implementation types (HTML vs HTTP header vs sitemap)

- **Check**  
  For each page or file, verify that hreflang is implemented using **one of the supported methods**:
  - HTML `<link rel="alternate" hreflang="…">` in `<head>` (for HTML pages),
  - `Link:` HTTP response headers (especially for **non‑HTML** content such as PDFs),
  - or XML **sitemaps** with `<xhtml:link rel="alternate" hreflang="…">`.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms that hreflang is implemented in a **method Google supports** and can consume.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – describes HTML `<link>`, HTTP header, and sitemap implementations  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 2. Hreflang syntax correctness

### 2.1. Correct use of `rel="alternate"` with `hreflang`

- **Check**  
  Verify that each hreflang annotation uses:
  - `rel="alternate"`, and
  - a `hreflang="value"` attribute, and
  - an `href="fully-qualified URL"`.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that the link is explicitly marked as an **alternate version** for a particular language/region and is a valid alternate URL.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “Add `<link rel="alternate" hreflang="lang_code" …>` elements to your page header…”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 2.2. Fully qualified URLs in hreflang

- **Check**  
  For each hreflang annotation, verify that `href` uses a **fully qualified URL** (scheme, host, and path), not a relative path.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms that Google can correctly identify the target URL for each localized version.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – examples and note on URLs being fully qualified  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 2.3. Valid language/region codes (ISO 639‑1 / ISO 3166‑1 Alpha‑2)

- **Check**  
  For every `hreflang` value:
  - Confirm that the **first subtag** is a valid **ISO 639‑1 language code**.
  - If a region is present, confirm that the **second subtag** is a valid **ISO 3166‑1 Alpha‑2 region code**.
  - Confirm **no region‑only codes** (such as `hreflang="US"` without a language).
  - Confirm all region codes used are **officially assigned** codes.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that language and region targeting uses **codes Google recognizes**, and prevents “incorrect language codes” and “incorrect region codes” issues which can cause hreflang to be ignored or misinterpreted.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “The first code of the `hreflang` attribute is the language code (in ISO 639‑1 format) followed by an optional second code that represents the region code (in ISO 3166‑1 Alpha 2 format)… Specifying the region alone is not valid. Make sure you're using officially assigned code elements…”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 2.4. Correct language–region order

- **Check**  
  Verify `hreflang` values are ordered as `language-region` (for example `en-US`, `fr-CA`) rather than `region-language` or any other order.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures hreflang values match Google’s **language first, optional region second** syntax, so that annotation is recognized.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – definition of `hreflang` value structure  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 3. Self‑referencing hreflang

### 3.1. Each page includes a self‑referencing hreflang entry

- **Check**  
  For each page in a localized set, verify that **the page references itself** in its own hreflang implementation:
  - In HTML: one `<link rel="alternate" hreflang="xx-YY" href="this-page-URL">` for the page itself.
  - In HTTP headers: one `Link:` header entry with the page’s own URL and its own `hreflang`.
  - In sitemaps: one `<xhtml:link>` entry where `href` equals the current `<loc>` URL and `hreflang` matches that page’s language/region.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms compliance with Google’s requirement that the hreflang set for a page **includes the requested version itself**, improving hreflang cluster consistency and avoiding partial sets.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “For each variation of the page, include a set of `<link>` elements in the `<head>` element, one link for each page variant **including itself**. The set of links is identical for every version of the page.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]  
  - For HTTP headers: “You must specify a set of `url`, `rel="alternate"`, and `hreflang` values for every version of the page **including the requested version**…”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 4. Return‑link reciprocity (bidirectional hreflang)

### 4.1. Every hreflang link is reciprocated

- **Check**  
  For each pair of pages A and B in a hreflang cluster:
  - If A has a hreflang annotation pointing to B, verify that B also has an hreflang annotation pointing to A.
  - Apply this for **all** variants in the set (that is, the hreflang set is **identical** on each variant).

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures **bidirectional linking** among all language/region variants. Google explicitly warns that **missing return links** may cause hreflang annotations to be ignored or misinterpreted.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – problem section “Missing return links: If page X links to page Y, page Y must link back to page X. If this is not the case for all pages that use `hreflang` annotations, those annotations may be ignored or not interpreted correctly.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 4.2. Identical hreflang sets across all variants

- **Check**  
  For a group of localized URLs representing the same content in different languages/regions, verify that the **set of hreflang entries** (same list of `hreflang` values and target URLs) is **identical on every page in the group**.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms that each variant participates in the same hreflang cluster, making it easier for Google to treat them as alternate versions.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “The set of links is identical for every version of the page.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 5. `x-default` usage

### 5.1. Valid `hreflang="x-default"` value

- **Check**  
  If an `x-default` URL is used:
  - Confirm the presence of at most one `hreflang="x-default"` per hreflang cluster.
  - Confirm that the `x-default` URL is included in the same hreflang set as other language/region URLs.
  - Confirm the `href` of the `x-default` entry is the URL intended for users whose language or region does not match any specific URL.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that `x-default` is properly implemented as the **fallback URL** for users whose language/region is not explicitly targeted, helping Google route such users correctly.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “To implement the `hreflang="x-default"` annotation, add an additional `link` tag to the existing `hreflang` annotations, and set the `href` attribute to the URL where you want your users to land if your site doesn't support their language.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 6. Hreflang in sitemaps

*(All checks also apply conceptually to HTML and HTTP headers; these are specifically sitemap-focused.)*

### 6.1. Correct sitemap `<xhtml:link>` structure

- **Check**  
  In XML sitemaps:
  - For each `<url>`, verify `<xhtml:link rel="alternate" hreflang="xx-YY" href="…">` entries are present for **all variants including itself**.
  - Ensure `rel="alternate"` and `hreflang` are correctly specified on each `<xhtml:link>`.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that hreflang in sitemaps follows the **supported format** and that Google can interpret localized relationships through the sitemap.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – XML sitemap implementation examples  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 6.2. Identical hreflang cluster across sitemap entries

- **Check**  
  For the set of `<url>` entries that represent alternate versions of the same content, verify that **each `<url>` entry lists the same set of `<xhtml:link>` hreflang annotations**, including itself.

- **What it verifies**  
  Confirms **reciprocity and completeness** of hreflang relationships when implemented via sitemaps.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – same principle of identical sets for each version, applied to sitemap examples  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 7. Hreflang in HTTP headers

### 7.1. Correct `Link:` header syntax

- **Check**  
  For each HTTP response that uses hreflang:
  - Verify that each `Link:` header entry is of the form  
    `Link: <URL>; rel="alternate"; hreflang="xx-YY"`  
    (potentially with multiple entries separated by commas).
  - Verify that the set of hreflang entries in HTTP headers mirrors the HTML/sitemap cluster if both are used for the same content (or that only one implementation is chosen per Google’s guidance for consistency).

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that hreflang implemented via HTTP headers is syntactically correct and includes all variants, including the requested version.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – HTTP headers implementation: “You must specify a set of `url`, `rel="alternate"`, and `hreflang` values for every version of the page including the requested version, separated by a comma as shown in the following example. The `Link:` header returned for every version of a page is identical.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 8. Conflicting or invalid hreflang annotations

Google doesn’t list every possible conflict pattern, but it does explicitly call out some common issues that are testable.

### 8.1. Missing return links (already covered, but explicitly an error condition)

- **Check**  
  Programmatically detect cases where:
  - Page A has hreflang to page B, but
  - Page B does **not** have hreflang back to page A.

- **What it verifies**  
  Identifies **“Missing return links”**, which Google documents as a problem that can cause hreflang annotations to be **ignored or not interpreted correctly**.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “Missing return links” section  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 8.2. Incorrect language codes

- **Check**  
  Identify any `hreflang` values where:
  - the first subtag is **not** a valid ISO 639‑1 language code, or
  - the code is malformed (for example, three letters, or not officially assigned).

- **What it verifies**  
  Detects **“Incorrect language codes”**, which Google describes as a problem that must be corrected.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “Incorrect language codes: Make sure that all language codes you use identify the language (in ISO 639‑1 format)… Specifying the region alone is not valid.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 8.3. Incorrect region codes

- **Check**  
  Identify any `hreflang` values:
  - whose second subtag, when present, is **not** a valid ISO 3166‑1 Alpha‑2 region code, or
  - that use deprecated or unofficial codes.

- **What it verifies**  
  Detects **“Incorrect region codes”** that Google specifically warns against.[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – “Incorrect region codes: Make sure you're using officially assigned code elements for the regions you're trying to identify (in ISO 3166‑1 Alpha 2 format).”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

### 8.4. Region‑only hreflang (invalid)

- **Check**  
  Detect any `hreflang` values that consist only of a region code (for example, `hreflang="US"` or `hreflang="GB"` without a language).

- **What it verifies**  
  Flags a configuration that Google explicitly says is **not valid**: “Specifying the region alone is not valid.”[4]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Localized versions of your pages* – statement on region‑only values being invalid  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/localized-versions`[4]

---

## 9. Internationalization requirements around URLs and content (contextual i18n SEO checks)

These are not hreflang syntax checks, but they are **Google i18n SEO requirements** that are testable and often audited together with hreflang.

### 9.1. Distinct URLs per language/region

- **Check**  
  Verify that different language/region versions of content use **separate URLs** (e.g., separate path, subdomain, or ccTLD), rather than changing language via cookies, JavaScript, or IP detection on the same URL.

- **What it verifies**  
  Complies with Google guidance that different language versions **should use different URLs**, which allows hreflang and indexing to work correctly.[6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Managing multi-regional and multilingual sites* – “If you use different URLs for different languages, use …” and related guidance about using different URLs for each language version  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/managing-multi-regional-sites`[6]

---

### 9.2. Avoid automatic redirection based on user language

- **Check**  
  Detect automatic redirection behaviors based on:
  - IP geolocation, or
  - `Accept-Language` headers, or
  - browser settings  
  that prevent users (and Googlebot) from **accessing all language versions**.

- **What it verifies**  
  Compliance with Google advice to **avoid automatically redirecting** users from one language version to another, since it can prevent Google from crawling all variants.[6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Managing multi-regional and multilingual sites* – “Avoid automatically redirecting users from one language version of a site to a different language version of a site.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/managing-multi-regional-sites`[6]

---

### 9.3. UTF‑8 and properly escaped URLs (when using localized URLs)

- **Check**  
  For localized URLs (e.g., URLs with non‑ASCII characters):
  - Verify that URLs are served and linked using **UTF‑8 encoding**.
  - Verify that URLs are **properly escaped** when included in links, sitemaps, and hreflang annotations.

- **What it verifies**  
  Ensures that internationalized URLs are **indexable and crawlable** and that hreflang references to those URLs are technically correct.[6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Managing multi-regional and multilingual sites* – “It’s fine to use localized words in the URL… However, be sure to use UTF‑8 encoding in the URL… and remember to escape the URLs properly when linking to them.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/managing-multi-regional-sites`[6]

---

### 9.4. Using hreflang or sitemaps for page‑level geotargeting

- **Check**  
  Where geotargeting is needed at page level:
  - Verify that **hreflang annotations or sitemaps** are used to indicate which pages apply to which locations or languages, rather than relying only on site‑level geotargeting.

- **What it verifies**  
  Aligns with Google’s guidance on **geotargeting** and how hreflang fits into signaling relevance by language/region.[6]

- **Search Central topic & URL**  
  - *Managing multi-regional and multilingual sites* – “To geotarget your site on Google… Page level: Use `hreflang` or sitemaps to tell Google which pages apply to which locations or languages.”  
    `https://developers.google.com/search/docs/specialty/international/managing-multi-regional-sites`[6]

---

If you’d like, I can turn this into a machine‑readable checklist (for example, JSON with check IDs, severity, and references) that you can plug directly into a QA/SEO testing specification.
